author: @nilton61
text:
You are so very very wrong. 
In the dc case the capacitor gets charged once. The only energy converted is the amount that is needed to charge the capacitor. 
If its an ac generator the capacitor get continuously charged and discharged which results in an ac current 90 degrees out of phase with the voltage. The energy is shuffled back and forth between the inductance of the generator and the capacitor. Even in this case no useful energy is produced except for covering for the Ri^2 losses caused for the current flowing. 
Can you please refrain from spreading disinformation and giving people that haven't got the facts stupid (and possibly expensive) ideas

author: @joejoejoejoejoejoe4391
text:
I can remember a documentary about some people who were trying to train captive chimps to live in the wild, so they could release them ( I can't help thinking that this would be like dumping a middle class Brit into the depths of inner-city Bronx).
 The made a concrete half ant hill with holes in and a jar of honey in the middle, and they showed the chimps how to use sticks to poke through the holes to get to the honey, but the most intelligent chimp just watched, as if it couldn't understand, much the the puzzlement of the people. Then it just reached around the half concrete anthill, and grabbed the whole pot of honey - proving it really was the most intelligent .

author: @joejoejoejoejoejoe4391
text: They did this with some kind of poultry; chickens or turkeys or something, they revealed a predator to half of a group, but a detergent bottle to the other half, because of the panic of the half that saw the predator, the other half that saw the detergent bottle associated the panic with that, and thus leaned to associate the bottle with danger.

author: @Ken00001010
text:
If there is a rotating source, a "mover" from which I want to extract power with a given generator, what I want to find out is what load will get the most power from that arrangement and how much power is that? For some powerful movers, all I need to test is the generator because the mover puts out so much torque that the load from the generator does not reduce its rate of rotation (RPM). In that case, the maximum power from the generator will occur into a load resistance that matches its internal winding impedance, at which half the power from the mover is going to heat the generator and half is going to the load. Testing that is easy by having a set of load resistors on a rotary switch and measuring the output voltage for each load value and plotting that squared divided by the resistor value. That plot gives you the power curve, also often used to characterize solar panels.

In the case of a small wind or water turbine plus generator, it is a bit more complex because the turbine is going to feel the load as a torque reducing its RPM through the gearing to the generator. At the extremes the gearing could be highly reducing and the generator would have very low RPM (low output voltage) but little torque dragging the turbine, or high step up gearing causing the generator to spin very fast giving high output voltage, but not be able to supply much current before the reverse torque stalls the turbine. It is easy to see that somewhere in the middle the output power available will peak, and that for a given mover and generator there is a gearing and load resistance that matches that maximum power point.

Can you characterize such a system by attaching a capacitor to it? Yes, to some extent that is going to work. (The following assumes a DC generator.  For AC, a bridge will be needed to convert to DC ahead of the capacitor.) When you switch it on the discharged capacitor is going to look like a dead short, and the generator is going to supply its maximum current (with maximum torque going back to the turbine) and its power is going to be heating the internal windings. As the capacitor starts to charge up, it looks to the generator like a resistive load where the resistance keeps going up. At the same time, the drag on the turbine is going down and its RPM is going up. At some time in the cap's charging it will look to the generator like a resistance equal to its own internal impedance, and that is the max power point. As the cap keeps charging, the generator will supply less and less current until it hits the max voltage it can supply and current stops flowing into the charged capacitor. If you put a recording meter on the cap that plots the current (falling down from start) into the cap and the voltage (going up from start), that plot will show that the product of current and voltage (power) will peak at some point. With some practice and calibration of test capacitors, you could get to the point where the length of time until the cap gets to 1/2 of its final charging voltage tells you what you want to know.

If you are powering your house off a wind generator, you are going to want to dynamically match the load so you get the most power at whatever changing wind speed. In the old mechanical days of windmills that had to be done with a variable transmission such as moving belts on and off different sized hubs (like a bicycle derailleur) but now that can, for a limited range, be done electronically by a device that acts like a variable transformer between the generator and the load. These devices have an internal processor that hunts for the setting that will transform the voltage and current from the generator into an output voltage and current that supplies maximum power to a fixed load, such as charging batteries. This is called a Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) controller and you can just buy them and attach them to a generator you want to test or you can build your own as an interesting microcontroller project.

author: @captainscarlet6758
text: I saw the same thing with a group of people in a room sitting on chairs. There was a beep and 1 person stood up. Every now and then the beep would go off and he would stand up then sit down.  As another person came in the room the same thing happened over and over again. As people were introduced in the room the beep would go off and gradually people would stand up. Even when the first person that started this off was removed.  He was in on this from the start. By the end of the experiment,  all the people in the room would stand up and sit down to the beep, but no one told them why they were doing it. 😂

author: @rickdworsky6457
text: The chimps got it right. Life is bigger, more encompassing, than climbing up slippery (corporate?) ladders for bananas. Obviously they weren't that hungry. And they had dignity.

replies.author: @joejoejoejoejoejoe4391
replies.text: If there was a hierarchy (I think there often is with chimps) the dominant ones would expect the lower ones to fetch the bananas; this would both get them the bananas, and reinforce the hierarchy. I think if there were female chimps, some male would fetch the bananas for them, for a "reward" - prostitution is not only the oldest profession, it seems that it's older than the human race.

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text: The irony is that humans think they are smarter than animals, yet many species of animals understand our speech while we don't understand theirs. They may not understand a full sentence but they can learn the meaning of dozens of individual words and intonations.

author: @silverpc4611
text: I was thinking that in a generator. In individual coils or serpentine coil of add capacitors. add it to every other loop of wire in serpentine for example. Would it improve performance.

replies.author: @rickdworsky6457
replies.text: There is only so much energy in the system. Adding the inherent resistance of the capicator would lose energy as waste heat, without any advantages. No component can ever be 100% efficient at transferring lower states of entropy. Certainly no free lunch. See: 2nd law of thermodynamics.
